text: The ancient Greek concept of 'scholē' — from which we derive 'school' — did not mean leisure in the modern sense of relaxation. It meant time deliberately freed from necessity, made available for thinking. Aristotle considered scholē the highest form of human activity: not because idleness is virtuous, but because only in non-instrumental time can the mind pursue understanding for its own sake. The tragedy of modernity, several philosophers have argued, is that we have abolished necessity without creating scholē — we have only created more elaborate divertissement, mistaking stimulation for freedom.
question: According to the passage, what did Aristotle mean by calling scholē the highest form of human activity?

options.text: That rest and recuperation are prerequisites for all productive intellectual work
That only in non-instrumental time can the mind pursue understanding for its own sake
That citizens who are freed from labor will naturally develop civic virtues
That idleness cultivates the kind of humility required for philosophical inquiry
